text: The surgeon mobilizes the distal segment of the bowel, which is scarred down in the pelvis, unfolding it from its previous position.

The surgeon mobilizes the distal segment of the bowel, which is scarred and folded within the pelvis, using robotic assistance. The segment is carefully unfolded and separated from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon mobilizes the proximal bowel to gain additional length, following the takedown and stapling off of the colostomy. A long silk thread is attached to the end of the colostomy for identification and traction. The colostomy site is used as access to the abdomen.

The surgeon mobilizes the proximal bowel to gain additional length, using the colostomy site for abdominal access. A silk thread is visible on the end of the bowel for identification and traction. The tongue of omentum is seen to the right, near the previous fistula site. The sigmoid loop is unfolded to further increase bowel length, eliminating tension. The surgeon prepares for a hand-sewn anastomosis, using a stay suture on the far corner and sewing the two staple lines together with a 3-0 V-lock suture.

The surgeon observes the silk thread used for traction and assesses the bowel length, noting the tongue of omentum positioned towards the previous fistula site. The sigmoid loop is unfolded to increase length, eliminating tension. The surgeon prepares for a hand-sewn anastomosis, using a stay suture on the far corner with 3-0 silk, and aligns the staple lines of the bowel ends, utilizing a 3-0 V-lock suture for the anastomosis.
The surgeon performs a colotomy, carefully checking for tissue fusion during the procedure. A diverting loop ileostomy is created due to prior radiation exposure, ensuring no complications arise.

The surgeon performs a colotomy and proctotomy, leaving some sigmoid tissue from the previous Hartman procedure. The proximal and distal holes are visible, and a 3-0 V-lock suture is used to sew from the center outward in a baseball stitch pattern, ensuring precise placement of each suture. The stitching continues around the corners, cinching down each suture, and then the canal is formed over the top.

The surgeon identifies the colotomy and proctotomy sites, noting the presence of remaining sigmoid tissue from the initial Hartmann procedure. The proximal and distal openings are visible, and a 3-0 V-Loc suture is used to begin a baseball stitch from the center, sewing outward around the openings.

The surgeon places each suture precisely around the far corner of the anastomosis site, then begins sewing from the middle towards themselves, cinching down each suture. The suturing continues around the near corner, followed by canalization over the top of the anastomosis.

The surgeon closes the anterior part of the enterotomies common channel using a single layer sewn technique, starting with the original inner stitch. The posterior outer row is sewn anteriorly across the top, securing the anastomosis. A rigid scope is used to check the distal fistula site, followed by a leak test in the rectum and vagina, both of which are negative. A flexible endoscopy confirms the anastomosis is widely patent and well perfused.

The surgeon closes the anterior part of the enterotomies' common channel using a single-layer sewn anastomosis technique. The original posterior outer row is sewn anteriorly across the top, securing the anastomosis. A rigid scope is used to check the distal fistula site, followed by a leak test in the rectum and vagina, both of which are negative. The anastomosis is confirmed to be widely patent and well perfused via flexible endoscopy.
